text: This is the case of an eighty-two year old patient affected by HCV liver fibrosis with a diagnosis of a single resectable two centimetre hepatocellular carcinoma of the superior border of segment two.
Preoperative workup included liver MRI, thoracic CT scan and blood exams. Findings were as follows.
Child PEWS score of A five, MELD score of eight, normal platelet count and AFP within normal values. Gastroscopy was normal.
After a multidisciplinary discussion, a liver resection was indicated using a robotic minimally invasive approach.
Four robotic ports are placed in a horizontal line two centimeters above the umbilicus, and a twelve millimeter assistant port is inserted under the umbilicus on the left side.
The surgeon exposes the hepatic pedicle and performs vascular control, followed by opening the lesser omentum.
The assistant passes a tape around the hepatic pedicle for clamping, and the tape is exteriorized extra-abdominally on the left side.

Intraoperative ultrasonography is used to localize the tumor and visualize its relationship with the ending of the left suprahepatic vein. The ultrasound probe is moved downward on the left lobe to identify the portal pedicles of segments two and three. The rest of the liver is scanned, revealing no additional lesions.

The surgeon dissects the left portion of the triangular ligament to expose the caval suprahepatic confluence, facilitating ultrasonography. The lesion is faintly visible at the inferior part of the screen.

The surgeon uses a hook and bipolar forceps to achieve dissection, while the assistant operates through an auxiliary port in the left iliac fossa. The pneumoperitoneum is maintained with an air seal system.
The caval suprahepatic confluence is visible. The surgeon marks the transection line using a hook, starting from the left border of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon performs intermittent clamping of the hepatic pedicle, alternating ten minutes of clamping with ten minutes of pause. Parenchymal transection is executed using cheliclasia with Maryland bipolar forceps, while the assistant irrigates the forceps during the procedure.
The robotic arm number three progressively opens and exposes the transection line. Kelly closure is used to selectively isolate the pedicles of liver segments two and three.
A small portal branch leading to the inferior part of liver segment three is identified. The surgeon places a hemlock clip on the branch and divides it using scissors.
The Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator (CUSA) is utilized through robotic arm number three to perform a precise dissection of the portal pedicles.
The surgeon places a hemlock clip on a vessel in the distal part, followed by coagulation using bipolar forceps to achieve division.
The surgeon continues the cranial transection, focusing on the proximal part to enhance the exposure of the pedicles in liver segments two and three.

The surgeon uses the Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator (CUSA) introduced through the auxiliary port, guided by robotic arm number three, to perform precise dissection of the branches leading to the left lobe of the liver.
The surgeon ensures haemostasis and biliostasis by applying a haemlock clip to the portal pedicle, which is progressively isolated as it heads towards liver segment three.

The surgeon opens and dissects the inferior part of the left lobe, passing a tape around the pedicle of liver segment three. The tape is tightened to reduce the pedicle's caliber, facilitating the placement of a hemlock clip.

The surgeon uses scissors to achieve vision while exerting traction on the thread to expose the pedicle of segment two, which is progressively dissected. The origin of the left suprahepatic vein is partially visible, coursing cephalad to the divided pedicle along the transection line.
The surgeon passes a thread around the Glissonian pedicle of segment two, tightens the tape, and places a Hemlock clip. The pedicle is then divided using scissors.
The surgeon progressively opens the transection line, exposing the lateral border of the left lobe. Dissection continues in the superior part using a hook instrument.
The surgeon isolates the suprahepatic confluence and opens and divides the left triangular ligament, further exposing the liver structures.

Intraoperative ultrasonography is used to assess the relationship between the lesion and the left suprahepatic vein. The surgeon dissects towards the medial part of segment two and along the border with segment four A. A small tear on the common trunk is sutured using a 6-0 polypropylene X-shaped stitch.

The surgeon isolates a segmental vein originating from segment two, revealing the end of the left suprahepatic vein at the level of the common trunk. The topographic relationship between the lesion and the hepatic vein is visible. The segmental vein from segment two is divided.
The surgeon uses a five-zero polypropylene suture to secure the proximal part of the surgical site.

The surgeon isolates the specimen, focusing on the terminal part of the left suprahepatic vein. A tape is placed around the vein to aid exposure, and a vascular clamp is applied proximally. The vein is then divided using scissors.
The surgeon frees the specimen completely and pushes it laterally. The stump of the left suprahepatic vein is sutured using a double five-zero polypropylene running suture.

A proximal and distal stitch is placed to aid in exposing the venous stump during suturing. The robotic arm number four applies traction on the proximal part of the running suture. The vascular clamp is opened at the end of the suture, and the closure of the venous stump is visually confirmed.

The specimen is introduced into a bag which is taken out through a phalanx steels incision. There were no intraoperative complications. The postoperative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged on postoperative day four. Pathological findings showed the presence of a PT-I well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma, Edmonds and Steiner grade one, with no vascular invasion, R-zero for complete resection.
